author: @robertcringely7348
text: A bit of advice from a guy who makes documentary films about technology: tell the viewer what it is you want them to notice in a scene. You have a tendency to throw some meter on a poorly-lighted desk, run an A-B test with it, then say, "Isn't that great?"  We can't know unless you tell us. We have no idea what the machine is, which line on the display to read (if we even CAN read it) and whether the number going up or going down is better. And that's all on you, Robert. It's the same way British textbooks use French language quotations without giving English translations. If you dumb it down a bit you'll reach a much larger audience with your otherwise excellent content.

replies.author: @Rob-vw3if
replies.text: I think you’ll find that the point of using fluorescent material is its ability to transform invisible light waves, which solar panels cannot into usable visible light. Note Robert explains how the edges of the acrylic sheet shine more brightly. As such, a clear prisms will only guide the light to the panel which can also be achieved by pointing your panel at the sun the fluorescent properties allows you to take advantage of light or radiation that would have no effect in normal circumstances. At least this is my understanding so please correct me if I’m wrong.

author: @collie147
text: There are plenty of guides to "printing glass" as it were. From memory it's print it with 10c+ over the recommended temp and increase the flow by a few percent so it melds together better. It won't be as smooth a finish with stringing and all but the idea is that the horizontal layers are still molten when the next layer is laid down and it's extruded almost into the previous layer so there's no air gap.

author: @gerryjamesedwards1227
text:
You could employ a bit of bio-mimicry here; from Cannabis plants, of all places. The stalked resin glands, called trichomes, are what give the female flowers that sugary-looking coating. They are tiny little structures, but utterly ingenious! The stalked part terminates at the top in a flat disc of cells and pores. The cells take in light from the sun and resin is exuded from the pores. The refractory properties of the resin combine with the viscosity to make the perfect collector of light on a static structure. A large proportion of the light in the more energetic end of the spectrum gets bent by the resin so that it hits the flat disc at the bottom of the globule of resin. The resin is completely opaque to UV, which means it's all getting used, something 99% of people growing the plant indoors don't realise.

The reason I bring this up is that there must be an analogous substance for making a light collector whose shape and index of refraction are such that it will bend the majority of the light that hits it onto the solar panel, without having to mold it, just let it form flat-bottomed beads all over the surface of the panel. Or, to increase the surface are even more, you could make one huge collector by copying the shape of a globule and scaling it up to fit a whole solar panel. The big drawback I can see straight away is the outlay for the kit to measure the index of refraction of stuff. I did see a second-hand instrument on the Ebay page of a lab gear recycler, and even that was a couple of thousand quid. It could be done much more basically, but you'd not be able to quantify the results. 

I'm trying to plan out a small setup for replicating Dr. Tour's Flash-Graphene process, which uses joule heating to turn carbon into turbostratic graphene by discharging a big capacitor bank through it. The absolute most expensive element in the shole setup is going to be testing the end product to confirm that is graphene in that config. It has to be done with Raman spectroscopy, really (I couldn't find any other method which was being used, but that might be my mistake), and means either sending samples off to a lab, renting a spectrosope or buying a second-hand one. There is the possibility of building a DIY contraption to get Raman lines, but you'd still have results that are open to question and it's only slightly cheaper than the other options. It would still cost more than  the capacitors for the bank, or the set-up for squeezing the starting carbon material and zapping it. At first they (and Tech Ingredients YT channel) thought that it had to be done under vacuum, but a very slight reduction in air-pressure is useful for pulling any gasses out afterwards, but that's not essential, apparently, so it really is a reasonable investment that you could realistically get back... as long as you can prove that the end result is graphene, of course.

Sorry to blather on, I won't be offended, or surprised, if nobody bothers reading that lot.

author: @josephpk4878
text: Interesting. I'm wondering whether a quick shot of liquid mirror on the back surface might improve the results. On a microscopic scale, this would act much like a fresnel lens - with the layer lines themselves creating individual convex lenses. If someone prints this out, I'd be inclined to over-extrude to something ridiculous - like 1 to 2mm, or more - to decrease the number of joint lines and create a more uniform interior structure for the light to pass through.

replies.author: @AnonymousAnarchist2
replies.text:
but not with the surface reflecting the light in front of the solar panal. like the diy light guide, and the industrial light guides, and the commertial light guides do.

Assuming of course the light source is "front".

And yes, he is demostraiting a commertial product, just in reverse of how it is most commonaly used, in most day to day applications its used in LCD T.V.'s and monitors with led's along the edge, the light gets spread out and makes a nice flat light source for a backlit LCD panel.
its also used for flat light sources for photography and reducing eye strain in office spaces.

And its one of the ways we currently manufacture solar windows; Windows that collect solar power, that and using thin film solar collecters that tint the incoming light. But those are only something youd come into contact with by building skyscrapers

replies.author: @jshaw4757
replies.text: ​I had an idea off mirror like edges around the pannel like say 5 inch fins like a baking tray sort off thing with the sides leaning outwards so your pannel is getting a couple inches more catching area and  these extra bits the top edge is lipped inwards so if you imagine like a bowl with a lipped edge inwards so when light shines into the extra area the lip bounces the light back onto the pannel...but this would obviosly be like square bowl sort off thing with pannel at the bottom...make them cheaply just ply board and mylar sheeting but also too this adding on top off this a piece off 2 way reflective mirror so the underside inside this box you have now essentially built the under side off this top sheet is reflective mirror but the top is normal glass so the light can get in and bounce around from the extra width you have added too this pannel...the other way I thought is underneath your panel a sheet off mylar that is say twice the size off your pannel then a similar box design build so that extra say 4 foot off mylar sheet underneath you put the box up around that so it's just catching more light basically like adding wings n then bouncing it back down as I said..be a cheap n cheerfull way off catching some extra rays...one more idea Rob if you don't mind would be rather than having panals horizontal on a roof have them verticle like in a box if you imagine a CD rack layed flat and each section has a vertical pannel stacked close together with the bottom tin foiled or mylared and one side off each pannel also mylared so it would essentially look  like a giant heat sink with all the light bouncing around inside....May look a bit unsightly on a roof but you could have them on the ground and have one off the pannels running a 50w led cob chip or pannel which switches on at night so charges through the night I mean if this box has the light bouncing around it may just harvest a bit more than what's going in due too you trapping the light cleverly but I'm sure this brakes some fundamental law about input in and energy out but if that's the case then use salt water too power small batts and use those too power LED light constantly day and night from salt water I've seen many LEDS running from salt so not a bad idea....anyway soz long message if any off that makes any sense lemme know...cheers mate

replies.text: Aha, I had an idea where the ends of such filaments could be distributed randomly and evenly all over the surface of a large solar panel, that way even if one area is shaded, the whole panel still gets some light from the distributed tangle of the filaments, preventing the old issue of an entire panel's output getting slashed by a little shaded area.
